displayTitle: Why Steel Grabs — and Aluminum Won't
kicker: MT LEVEL I // THEORY OF MAGNETISM

beats.narration: Why does steel grab magnetic particles while aluminum ignores them? The answer lives at the atomic scale and decides what you can test.

payload.title: INSIDE MAGNETISM
payload.sub: Why steel grabs particles and aluminum never will.

beats.narration: Steel is packed with tiny magnets: domains. Unmagnetized means scrambled, not empty. Apply a magnetizing force and they line up, giving the part poles.
Materials come in three families. Ferromagnetic: strongly attracted, the only class MT can test. Paramagnetic: barely attracted. Diamagnetic: feebly repelled.
Like poles repel, unlike attract. Crack a magnetized part and you create a pole pair that grabs particles into a crisp indication over the flaw.

payload.heading: A crack makes a pole pair

beats.narration: So here's the rule: no domains, no MT. Not ferromagnetic? The answer is a different method, never a higher amperage.

payload.sub: A different method — never a higher amperage.
